examples.explanation: Triangle: [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7], [4,1,8,3]]. Call min_path(0,0): triangle[0][0]=2. Recursively: min_path(1,0) and min_path(1,1). min_path(1,0): triangle[1][0]=3. Recursively: min_path(2,0) and min_path(2,1). min_path(2,0): triangle[2][0]=6. Recursively: min_path(3,0) and min_path(3,1). min_path(3,0): base case (last row), return 4. min_path(3,1): return 1. So min_path(2,0)=6+min(4,1)=6+1=7. min_path(2,1): triangle[2][1]=5. Recursively: min_path(3,1) and min_path(3,2). min_path(3,1): return 1. min_path(3,2): return 8. So min_path(2,1)=5+min(1,8)=5+1=6. Thus min_path(1,0)=3+min(7,6)=3+6=9. min_path(1,1): triangle[1][1]=4. Recursively: min_path(2,1) and min_path(2,2). min_path(2,1)=6 (from above). min_path(2,2): triangle[2][2]=7. Recursively: min_path(3,2) and min_path(3,3). min_path(3,2)=8. min_path(3,3)=3. So min_path(2,2)=7+min(8,3)=7+3=10. Thus min_path(1,1)=4+min(6,10)=4+6=10. Finally, min_path(0,0)=2+min(9,10)=2+9=11. Minimum path: 2→3→5→1 = 11.

Triangle: [[-1], [2,3], [1,-1,-3]]. Call min_path(0,0): triangle[0][0]=-1. Recursively: min_path(1,0) and min_path(1,1). min_path(1,0): triangle[1][0]=2. Recursively: min_path(2,0) and min_path(2,1). min_path(2,0): base case, return 1. min_path(2,1): return -1. So min_path(1,0)=2+min(1,-1)=2+(-1)=1. min_path(1,1): triangle[1][1]=3. Recursively: min_path(2,1) and min_path(2,2). min_path(2,1)=-1. min_path(2,2): return -3. So min_path(1,1)=3+min(-1,-3)=3+(-3)=0. Thus min_path(0,0)=-1+min(1,0)=-1+0=-1. Minimum path: -1→3→-3 = -1.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible paths from the top to the bottom of the triangle, calculating the sum for each path and returning the minimum. This approach is straightforward but highly inefficient for larger triangles due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Triangle Minimum Path Sum via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all paths from top to bottom to find minimum sum.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves the triangle minimum path sum problem by systematically exploring every possible path from the top element to the bottom row. The intuition is straightforward: starting from the top, at each position (i, j) in the triangle, you can move to either the element directly below (i+1, j) or diagonally below to the right (i+1, j+1). By recursively exploring both options for every element, we generate all possible paths through the triangle. Each path corresponds to a unique sequence of moves from top to bottom, and we calculate the sum of elements along each path. The minimum among all these sums is our answer. This approach works because it's exhaustive—it considers every possible way to traverse the triangle, guaranteeing we'll find the optimal path. However, this completeness comes at a cost: for a triangle with n rows, there are 2^(n-1) possible paths, leading to exponential time complexity. The algorithm begins with a helper function min_path(i, j) that returns the minimum path sum starting from position (i, j). For the base case, when i equals the last row index, we simply return triangle[i][j] since there's nowhere else to go. For the recursive case, we return triangle[i][j] plus the minimum of min_path(i+1, j) and min_path(i+1, j+1). The main function calls min_path(0, 0) to start from the top. Time complexity is O(2^n) because each call generates two recursive calls, creating a binary tree of calls with depth n. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This connects directly to L1's helper function, which implements the core recursion. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (off-by-one errors with row indices), not considering both move options, or inefficient implementation that recalculates the same subproblems repeatedly (though memoization would change the approach). The brute force nature makes it impractical for large triangles but excellent for understanding the problem structure.

examples.trace: Triangle: [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7], [4,1,8,3]]. Call min_path(0,0): triangle[0][0]=2. Recursively: min_path(1,0) and min_path(1,1). min_path(1,0): triangle[1][0]=3. Recursively: min_path(2,0) and min_path(2,1). min_path(2,0): triangle[2][0]=6. Recursively: min_path(3,0) and min_path(3,1). min_path(3,0): base case (last row), return 4. min_path(3,1): return 1. So min_path(2,0)=6+min(4,1)=6+1=7. min_path(2,1): triangle[2][1]=5. Recursively: min_path(3,1) and min_path(3,2). min_path(3,1): return 1. min_path(3,2): return 8. So min_path(2,1)=5+min(1,8)=5+1=6. Thus min_path(1,0)=3+min(7,6)=3+6=9. min_path(1,1): triangle[1][1]=4. Recursively: min_path(2,1) and min_path(2,2). min_path(2,1)=6 (from above). min_path(2,2): triangle[2][2]=7. Recursively: min_path(3,2) and min_path(3,3). min_path(3,2)=8. min_path(3,3)=3. So min_path(2,2)=7+min(8,3)=7+3=10. Thus min_path(1,1)=4+min(6,10)=4+6=10. Finally, min_path(0,0)=2+min(9,10)=2+9=11. Minimum path: 2→3→5→1 = 11.

Triangle: [[-1], [2,3], [1,-1,-3]]. Call min_path(0,0): triangle[0][0]=-1. Recursively: min_path(1,0) and min_path(1,1). min_path(1,0): triangle[1][0]=2. Recursively: min_path(2,0) and min_path(2,1). min_path(2,0): base case, return 1. min_path(2,1): return -1. So min_path(1,0)=2+min(1,-1)=2+(-1)=1. min_path(1,1): triangle[1][1]=3. Recursively: min_path(2,1) and min_path(2,2). min_path(2,1)=-1. min_path(2,2): return -3. So min_path(1,1)=3+min(-1,-3)=3+(-3)=0. Thus min_path(0,0)=-1+min(1,0)=-1+0=-1. Minimum path: -1→3→-3 = -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for Path Sum from a Position
ladders.desc: Implement min_path(i, j) that returns minimum sum starting from triangle[i][j] to bottom.
ladders.explanation: The min_path(i, j) function is the heart of the brute force recursion approach. It computes the minimum path sum starting from a specific position (i, j) in the triangle down to the bottom row. The intuition is recursive decomposition: the minimum path from (i, j) equals the value at (i, j) plus the minimum of the paths starting from the two possible next positions—directly below (i+1, j) and diagonally below right (i+1, j+1). This works because any path from (i, j) must go through one of these two next steps, and by taking the minimum of the subsequent paths, we ensure optimality from that point onward. The function uses a base case when i is at the last row (i == len(triangle)-1), where there are no more moves, so it simply returns triangle[i][j]. Otherwise, it recursively calls itself for both child positions and returns triangle[i][j] + min(min_path(i+1, j), min_path(i+1, j+1)). This recursive structure mirrors the problem's tree-like decision process. Time complexity for this sub-routine alone is O(2^(n-i)) in the worst case, as it explores all paths from depth i to the bottom. Space complexity is O(n-i) for the recursion stack. This sub-routine directly feeds into L0 by being called from the top position (0,0) to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include forgetting the base case, incorrectly indexing the triangle (especially with j bounds), or not handling single-row triangles. The pseudocode pattern is straightforward but must be implemented carefully to avoid infinite recursion.

examples.trace: Call min_path(1,0) on triangle [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7]]. triangle[1][0]=3. Not last row (i=1, last row i=2). Recursively: min_path(2,0) and min_path(2,1). min_path(2,0): i=2 is last row, return triangle[2][0]=6. min_path(2,1): return triangle[2][1]=5. So min_path(1,0)=3+min(6,5)=3+5=8. Wait, correction: Let's trace carefully. Triangle rows: 0:[2], 1:[3,4], 2:[6,5,7]. min_path(1,0): value=3. Recursive calls: min_path(2,0)=6 (base), min_path(2,1)=5 (base). min(6,5)=5. So result=3+5=8. But earlier I said 9, that was an error. Correct output is 8. Path from (1,0): 3→5 = 8.

Call min_path(1,1) on triangle [[-1], [2,3], [1,-1,-3]]. triangle[1][1]=3. Not last row (i=1, last row i=2). Recursively: min_path(2,1) and min_path(2,2). min_path(2,1): base case, return triangle[2][1]=-1. min_path(2,2): return triangle[2][2]=-3. min(-1,-3)=-3. So min_path(1,1)=3+(-3)=0. Path from (1,1): 3→-3 = 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decision: Choose Minimum of Two Child Paths
ladders.desc: At each step, add current value to the minimum of recursive calls for two possible next positions.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force recursion is the decision-making process at each position in the triangle. For any element at row i and column j (where i is not the last row), the minimum path sum from that point is computed as: current value + min(path from left child, path from right child). Here, 'left child' refers to the element directly below at (i+1, j), and 'right child' refers to the element diagonally below at (i+1, j+1). The intuition is greedy at the recursive level: to minimize the total sum from the current position, we should choose the next step that leads to the smaller subsequent path sum. This works because the problem has optimal substructure—the optimal solution from a given point depends only on the optimal solutions from its reachable next points. By recursively applying this min operation, we ensure that at each decision point, we pick the better of the two possible futures. The algorithm doesn't actually make a greedy choice upfront; instead, it explores both futures recursively and uses min to select the better one after computing them. This is a form of divide-and-conquer where the problem is broken down into smaller subproblems (paths from child positions). The pseudocode for this logic is: if i == last_row: return triangle[i][j]; else: return triangle[i][j] + min(min_path(i+1, j), min_path(i+1, j+1)). Time complexity for this decision at one node is O(1) plus the time to solve subproblems, but overall it leads to exponential growth. Space complexity involves recursion depth. This logic is central to L1's helper function, as it defines the recursive relation. Common mistakes include swapping the min arguments, incorrect child indices, or not adding the current value. Understanding this core logic is key to grasping why the brute force approach, while inefficient, correctly solves the problem.

examples.input: Current value=3, left child path sum=7, right child path sum=6
examples.trace: Given current value = 3, left child path sum = 7, right child path sum = 6. Apply core logic: result = current value + min(left, right) = 3 + min(7, 6) = 3 + 6 = 9. The min operation chooses the smaller child path (6), so the optimal path from this point goes through the right child.

examples.input: Current value=-1, left child path sum=2, right child path sum=-3
examples.trace: Given current value = -1, left child path sum = 2, right child path sum = -3. Apply core logic: result = current value + min(left, right) = -1 + min(2, -3) = -1 + (-3) = -4. The min operation chooses -3, so the path goes through the right child for a lower total.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing Triangle Elements with Row and Column Indices
ladders.desc: Correctly index into the triangular array structure given row i and column j.
ladders.explanation: A fundamental building block for the triangle path sum problem is understanding how to access elements in the triangular data structure. The triangle is typically represented as a list of lists, where the first list has 1 element, the second has 2 elements, and so on, up to the nth row having n elements. Each row i (0-indexed) contains i+1 elements. To access a specific element, we use two indices: i for the row and j for the column within that row. The column index j must satisfy 0 ≤ j ≤ i, meaning it ranges from 0 to i inclusive. This triangular structure is crucial because it defines the valid moves: from position (i, j), you can only move to (i+1, j) or (i+1, j+1), provided i+1 is within bounds. The intuition is that the triangle represents a directed graph where each node connects to two nodes in the next row, maintaining the triangular shape. Accessing elements correctly ensures we follow the problem constraints and avoid index errors. In pseudocode, we access an element as triangle[i][j]. When implementing recursion, we must check that i and j are within valid ranges: i from 0 to n-1, and for a given i, j from 0 to i. The base case often involves checking if i equals n-1 (last row). This building block feeds into L2's core logic by providing the values needed for the min operation. Common mistakes include using j beyond i (e.g., accessing triangle[1][2] in a row with only 2 elements), misindexing rows (off-by-one), or not handling empty triangles. Practicing this indexing is essential before tackling the recursive logic.

examples.trace: Given triangle = [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7]]. Row i=1 has 2 elements (indices 0 and 1). Column j=1 is valid since 1 ≤ i (1 ≤ 1). Access triangle[1][1]: first index 1 selects row [3,4], second index 1 selects element 4. So output is 4.
Triangle = [[-1], [2,3], [1,-1,-3]]. Row i=2 has 3 elements (indices 0,1,2). Column j=0 is valid (0 ≤ 2). Access triangle[2][0]: row [1,-1,-3], index 0 gives 1. Output is 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding Two Numbers and Taking Minimum
ladders.desc: Perform arithmetic addition and comparison to find the smaller of two values.
ladders.explanation: At the most basic level, the triangle path sum problem relies on two fundamental operations: addition and comparison (specifically, finding the minimum). Addition is used to accumulate the sum of values along a path. For example, if the current path sum is S and we encounter a new value V, the updated sum is S + V. In the recursive approach, this appears as adding the current triangle element to the result from child paths. Comparison, via the min operation, is used to decide between two possible future paths. Given two numbers a and b, min(a, b) returns the smaller value. This is crucial for making the optimal choice at each step. The intuition is simple: to minimize the total, we always want to take the path with the smaller subsequent sum. These operations are combined in expressions like current_value + min(left_sum, right_sum). In programming, addition is straightforward with the + operator, and min is often a built-in function or can be implemented with an if statement. Time complexity for these operations is O(1), as they involve constant-time arithmetic and comparisons. Space complexity is negligible. This basic operation feeds into L3 by being used when accessing and processing triangle elements. Common mistakes include integer overflow (though rare in practice), incorrect order in min (e.g., min(right, left) is fine), or not handling negative numbers correctly (addition and min work normally with negatives). Beginners should practice these operations to build confidence before tackling more complex logic.

examples.input: Add 5 and 3, then find min of 8 and 10
examples.trace: First operation: addition. 5 + 3 = 8. Second operation: comparison. min(8, 10). Since 8 < 10, min returns 8. So output is 8.

examples.input: Add -2 and 7, then find min of 5 and -5
examples.trace: Addition: -2 + 7 = 5. Comparison: min(5, -5). Since -5 < 5, min returns -5. Output is -5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Number and Basic List Indexing
ladders.desc: Recognize numbers and access elements in a list using indices.
ladders.explanation: The absolute foundation for the triangle path sum problem is understanding two basic concepts: numbers and list indexing. Numbers are the fundamental data we work with; in this problem, they are integers that can be positive, negative, or zero. We need to be able to read, store, and manipulate numbers. List indexing is how we organize and access these numbers in a structured way. A list is an ordered collection of elements, where each element has an index starting from 0. For example, in the list [2, 3, 4], the element at index 0 is 2, at index 1 is 3, and at index 2 is 4. Accessing an element uses square brackets: list[index]. This is crucial because the triangle is represented as a list of lists—a two-dimensional structure where each row is a list. To access a specific number in the triangle, we first select the row list using the first index, then the element within that row using the second index. For instance, if triangle = [[2], [3,4]], then triangle[0][0] gives 2, and triangle[1][1] gives 4. The intuition is that indexing allows us to navigate the triangular grid. This foundation feeds into L4 by enabling the basic operations on these accessed values. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not in programming), accessing indices out of range (e.g., index 2 in a list of length 2), or confusing rows and columns. Beginners should practice creating lists, accessing elements, and understanding zero-based indexing before moving to more complex data structures.

examples.trace: Given list = [10, 20, 30]. Indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Access index 1: list[1] = 20. So output is 20.

examples.input: List of lists: [[5], [6,7]], row index: 1, column index: 0
examples.trace: Given list of lists: [[5], [6,7]]. First, access row index 1: this gives the second list [6,7]. Then, within this list, access column index 0: [6,7][0] = 6. So output is 6.

approaches.name: Top-Down Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store the minimum path sum for each position in the triangle, avoiding redundant calculations. This approach improves efficiency by caching results from overlapping subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Triangle Minimum Path Sum with Top-Down DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find the minimum path sum from top to bottom of a triangle.
ladders.explanation: The triangle minimum path sum problem requires finding the minimum sum of numbers along a path from the top to the bottom of a triangular array, where you can only move to adjacent numbers on the row below. The top-down dynamic programming approach with memoization solves this by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems. The intuition is that the minimum path sum to reach any position (i, j) in the triangle is the value at that position plus the minimum of the two possible paths from above (from (i-1, j-1) or (i-1, j)). By using recursion, we can explore all paths, but without memoization, this would be exponential. Memoization stores computed results in a 2D array to avoid redundant calculations, reducing time complexity to O(n²) where n is the number of rows. The algorithm starts from the top element and recursively computes the minimum sum for each position, caching results. The base case is at the bottom row, where the path sum is just the triangle value. The space complexity is O(n²) for the memo table. This approach is efficient because it leverages the optimal substructure property—the optimal solution to the whole problem depends on optimal solutions to subproblems. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like single-row triangles or incorrect indexing for adjacent positions. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the helper function for recursive computation with memoization. Real code pattern involves defining a recursive function with parameters for row and column indices, checking the memo table, computing the result, and storing it.

examples.trace: Triangle: [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7], [4,1,8,3]]. Start at top (0,0) with value 2. Recursively compute: For (1,0): value 3 + min(path from (0,0)=2) = 5. For (1,1): value 4 + min(path from (0,0)=2) = 6. For (2,0): value 6 + min(path from (1,0)=5) = 11. For (2,1): value 5 + min(path from (1,0)=5, (1,1)=6) = 10. For (2,2): value 7 + min(path from (1,1)=6) = 13. For (3,0): value 4 + min(path from (2,0)=11) = 15. For (3,1): value 1 + min(path from (2,0)=11, (2,1)=10) = 11. For (3,2): value 8 + min(path from (2,1)=10, (2,2)=13) = 18. For (3,3): value 3 + min(path from (2,2)=13) = 16. Minimum at bottom row: min(15,11,18,16) = 11. Return 11.
Triangle: [[-10]]. Only one row, so the minimum path sum is just the value at (0,0) = -10. No recursion needed as base case returns the value directly. Return -10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Recursive Helper Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes minimum path sum for a given position in the triangle.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the top-down DP approach, responsible for calculating the minimum path sum to reach a specific cell (i, j) in the triangle. The intuition is that to find the best path to (i, j), we need to consider the two possible parent cells above it: (i-1, j-1) and (i-1, j), take the minimum of their path sums, and add the current cell's value. The function uses memoization to store results in a 2D array (memo), avoiding recomputation. The algorithm: if i is 0 (top row), return triangle[0][0]; if j is out of bounds (less than 0 or greater than i), return infinity (or a large number) to ignore invalid paths; check memo[i][j] for cached result; if not cached, recursively compute result = triangle[i][j] + min(helper(i-1, j-1), helper(i-1, j)); store result in memo[i][j]; return result. Time complexity for this function, when integrated, is O(1) per call with memoization, but overall it drives O(n²) total calls. Space complexity is O(n²) for the memo table. This feeds into L0 by being called for each cell to build the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases or not initializing memo properly. Real code pattern involves a nested function with parameters i and j, using a 2D list for memo initialized with None.

examples.trace: Compute for (2,1): value 5. Check parents: (1,0) value 3, (1,1) value 4. Recursively: For (1,0): value 3 + min(path from (0,0)=2) = 5. For (1,1): value 4 + min(path from (0,0)=2) = 6. min(5,6) = 5. Result = 5 + 5 = 10. Store in memo[2][1] = 10.
Compute for (1,0): value 2. Parents: (0,0) value 1, (0,-1) invalid so return infinity. min(1, infinity) = 1. Result = 2 + 1 = 3. Store in memo[1][0] = 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Two Paths with Recursion
ladders.desc: The central technique of taking the minimum of two recursive calls and adding the current value.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how to compute the minimum path sum for a position by considering the two possible incoming paths. The intuition is based on the problem's structure: from any cell (i, j), you can only come from (i-1, j-1) or (i-1, j) in the row above. Therefore, the optimal path to (i, j) must be the better of the two optimal paths to those parent cells, plus the current cell's value. This exhibits optimal substructure, a key property for dynamic programming. The algorithm step is: result = triangle[i][j] + min(recursive_call(i-1, j-1), recursive_call(i-1, j)). This is applied recursively until reaching the top row. Time complexity for this logic in isolation is O(2^n) without memoization, but with memoization it becomes O(1) per cell. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack depth. This feeds into L1 as the main computation within the helper function. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases where j-1 or j might be out of bounds. Real code pattern uses a simple expression with min() and recursive calls.

examples.trace: Given current value = 5, parent sums are 3 and 4. Compute min(3,4) = 3. Add current value: 5 + 3 = 8. This is the minimum path sum through this cell.

examples.input: Current value: -2, Parent sums: 1 and infinity
examples.trace: Given current value = -2, parent sums are 1 and infinity (for invalid path). min(1, infinity) = 1. Add current value: -2 + 1 = -1. This handles edge cases gracefully.

testCases.input: Current value: 3, Parent sums: 1 and infinity
Current value: 6, Parent sums: infinity and 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implement recursion to traverse the triangle, handling base cases for the top row and out-of-bounds indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up a recursive function that navigates the triangle's rows and columns. The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem into smaller subproblems: to compute the path sum at a position, we need to compute sums for positions above it. The base cases are crucial: when at the top row (i=0), the path sum is just the triangle value, as there are no parents; when column index j is out of bounds (j < 0 or j > i), return a large value (like infinity) to indicate an invalid path, ensuring it's not chosen by min(). The algorithm: if i == 0: return triangle[0][0]; if j < 0 or j > i: return infinity; otherwise, proceed with recursive calls. Time complexity is exponential without optimization, but this block focuses on the recursive structure. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the recursive framework for the core logic. Common mistakes include missing base cases or incorrect infinity handling. Real code pattern uses if-conditions at the start of the recursive function.

examples.trace: At position (0,0): i=0, so base case triggers. Return triangle[0][0] = 2. No recursion needed.
At position (1,2): i=1, j=2. Check j > i (2 > 1), so out of bounds. Return infinity to mark invalid path.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Triangle Elements and Adjacent Indices
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations to retrieve values from the triangle and compute indices for adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with the triangle data structure, which is typically a list of lists where each row has one more element than the previous. The intuition is that to compute path sums, we need to correctly access the current cell's value and determine the indices of the two possible parent cells above. For a cell at row i and column j, the value is triangle[i][j]. The parents are at (i-1, j-1) and (i-1, j). It's essential to ensure indices are within bounds: i ranges from 0 to n-1, and j ranges from 0 to i within row i. The algorithm steps: given i and j, check if i and j are valid; if valid, access triangle[i][j]; compute parent indices as above. Time complexity is O(1) per access. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the data manipulation needed for recursion. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors or assuming rectangular arrays. Real code pattern uses simple list indexing and arithmetic for indices.

examples.trace: For position (2,1): row i=2, column j=1. Access triangle[2][1] = 5. Compute parent indices: (i-1, j-1) = (1,0) and (i-1, j) = (1,1). Values at parents: triangle[1][0]=3, triangle[1][1]=4.
For position (1,0): i=1, j=0. Access triangle[1][0] = 2. Compute parent indices: (0,-1) and (0,0). (0,-1) is out of bounds, so handle accordingly.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Triangle as a 2D List
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: representing and navigating a triangular array using lists in Python.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the data structure used in the problem: a triangle represented as a list of lists, where each inner list corresponds to a row, and the number of elements increases by one each row. The intuition is that this structure mimics a real-world triangle shape, allowing us to model the problem computationally. For example, a triangle with 3 rows might be [[a], [b,c], [d,e,f]]. Key operations include: accessing an element at row i and column j using triangle[i][j], where j must be between 0 and i inclusive; iterating through rows with a loop; understanding that row indices start at 0. This is essential because the problem's constraints (moving to adjacent numbers) rely on this triangular indexing. Time and space complexity for basic access is O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic data manipulation skills. Common mistakes include treating it as a rectangular 2D array or misindexing. Real code pattern involves defining a list of lists and using nested loops or direct indexing.

examples.trace: Given triangle = [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7]]. This has 3 rows. Row 0 has 1 element: index 0,0 value 2. Row 1 has 2 elements: indices (1,0)=3, (1,1)=4. Row 2 has 3 elements: indices (2,0)=6, (2,1)=5, (2,2)=7. Access example: triangle[1][1] returns 4.
Given triangle = [[-10]]. This has 1 row. Row 0 has 1 element: index 0,0 value -10. Access example: triangle[0][0] returns -10.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming Iterative O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the bottom row to the top, updating each element with the minimum sum from its two children below, ultimately finding the minimum path sum at the top. This method eliminates recursion overhead and is efficient for all triangle sizes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Triangle Minimum Path Sum via Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bottom-up dynamic programming to find the minimum path sum from top to bottom of a triangle.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to work from the bottom of the triangle upward, eliminating the need for recursion and reducing the problem to simple comparisons. Starting at the second-to-last row, each element is updated by adding the smaller of its two adjacent children from the row below. This process propagates upward, with each element representing the minimum path sum from that position to the bottom. By the time we reach the top, the single element contains the overall minimum path sum. This works because at each step, we're making optimal local decisions that guarantee a globally optimal solution due to the overlapping subproblems property of dynamic programming. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Create a copy of the triangle to avoid modifying the input. 2) Iterate from the second-to-last row up to the top row. 3) For each element in the current row, add the minimum of the two adjacent elements from the row below. 4) The top element now contains the answer. Time complexity is O(n²) where n is the number of rows, as we process each element once. Space complexity is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This full solution implements the key update subroutine repeatedly across all rows. Common mistakes include: modifying the input triangle directly (which can cause issues), incorrect indexing when accessing children elements, and not handling edge cases like single-row triangles. Real code pattern: def minimumTotal(triangle): dp = [row[:] for row in triangle]; for i in range(len(dp)-2, -1, -1): for j in range(len(dp[i])): dp[i][j] += min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]); return dp[0][0].

examples.trace: Triangle: [[-10]]. Step 1: Copy to dp: [[-10]]. Step 2: Only one row, so no iteration needed. Return dp[0][0]=-10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Update Row with Minimum Children
ladders.desc: The core update operation that modifies a row based on the row below.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the bottom-up DP approach. The intuition is that to find the minimum path from any position, we need to know the minimum paths from its two possible next positions (children). By updating each element in a row to be its original value plus the minimum of its two children from the row below, we effectively compute the best path from that position downward. This works because it breaks the problem into smaller, independent subproblems that can be solved in reverse order. Step-by-step: 1) Take a current row (list of integers) and the row directly below it (list of integers). 2) For each index j in the current row, compute current[j] + min(below[j], below[j+1]). 3) Store or return the updated current row. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the current row. Space complexity is O(1) if modifying in-place, or O(m) if creating a new row. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main loop to build up the solution. Common mistakes: accessing indices out of bounds (ensure below has length current_length+1), not understanding that each element in the current row corresponds to two children in the row below, and incorrect order of operations (addition before min or vice versa). Real code pattern: def updateRow(current, below): for j in range(len(current)): current[j] += min(below[j], below[j+1]); return current.

examples.trace: Step 1: For j=0: current[0]=3, min(below[0]=6, below[1]=5)=5 → 3+5=8? Wait, correction: Actually in the full problem, we add the minimum child. Let's trace properly: current=[3,4], below=[6,5,7]. For j=0: current[0]=3, min(below[0]=6, below[1]=5)=5 → 3+5=8. For j=1: current[1]=4, min(below[1]=5, below[2]=7)=5 → 4+5=9. So output should be [8,9]. But in the L0 example, we had [9,10] because below row was already updated. Let's use a consistent example: Suppose below row is original [6,5,7]. Then: For j=0: 3+min(6,5)=3+5=8. For j=1: 4+min(5,7)=4+5=9. Output: [8,9]. To match L0, if below row is [7,6,10] (updated), then: For j=0: 3+min(7,6)=3+6=9. For j=1: 4+min(6,10)=4+6=10. Output: [9,10]. I'll use the first for clarity: Input: current=[3,4], below=[6,5,7]. Output: [8,9]. Trace: j=0: 3+min(6,5)=3+5=8; j=1: 4+min(5,7)=4+5=9.
Step 1: For j=0: current[0]=2, min(below[0]=3, below[1]=4)=3 → 2+3=5. Output: [5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Two Adjacent Elements
ladders.desc: Finding the minimum between two adjacent elements in a list, a fundamental comparison in the DP update.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves comparing two adjacent elements in a list and selecting the smaller one. The intuition is that in the triangle path problem, from any position, you can move to one of two adjacent positions in the next row, so choosing the minimum child ensures the path sum is minimized locally. This is a greedy choice that, when applied bottom-up, leads to a global optimum due to the structure of the problem. The step-by-step is straightforward: given a list and an index j, compare list[j] and list[j+1], return the smaller value. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). Connection to L1: This operation is used within the updateRow subroutine for each element. Common mistakes: comparing non-adjacent elements, handling indices incorrectly (e.g., j+1 out of bounds), or not considering that the list represents a row in the triangle where each element has two children. Real code pattern: def minAdjacent(row, j): return min(row[j], row[j+1]).

examples.trace: Step 1: At index 0, compare row[0]=6 and row[1]=5. min(6,5)=5.
Step 1: At index 2, compare row[2]=8 and row[3]=3. min(8,3)=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over a Triangle in Reverse
ladders.desc: Looping through the rows of a triangle from bottom to top, a key control flow pattern.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over the rows of a triangle (list of lists) in reverse order, from the last row to the first. The intuition is that bottom-up dynamic programming requires solving subproblems from the base case upward, so we start at the bottom where paths are trivial (each element is itself) and work upwards. This reverse iteration ensures that when processing a row, the row below has already been computed. Step-by-step: 1) Determine the number of rows n. 2) Use a for loop with index i from n-2 down to 0 (since the last row doesn't need updating). 3) In each iteration, access triangle[i] as the current row and triangle[i+1] as the row below. Time complexity O(n) for the outer loop, space O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration provides the context for applying the minAdjacent logic across all rows. Common mistakes: incorrect range (e.g., going from n-1 to 0 which processes the last row unnecessarily), off-by-one errors, or not handling single-row triangles. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(triangle)-2, -1, -1): current = triangle[i]; below = triangle[i+1]; ...

examples.output: Rows processed in order: row 2, row 1, row 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Number of rows n=4. Step 2: Loop i from 2 to 0. Iteration 1: i=2, current row=[6,5,7], below row=[4,1,8,3]. Iteration 2: i=1, current row=[3,4], below row=[6,5,7] (updated in previous step). Iteration 3: i=0, current row=[2], below row=[3,4] (updated).
Step 1: n=2. Step 2: Loop i from 0 to 0. Iteration: i=0, current row=[1], below row=[2,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Elements in a List of Lists
ladders.desc: Retrieving elements from a nested list structure, fundamental for working with triangular data.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing elements in a list of lists, which represents the triangle. Each inner list is a row, and elements within are accessed by row and column indices. The intuition is that the triangle is stored as a jagged array where row i has i+1 elements. Proper indexing is crucial for navigating parent-child relationships. Step-by-step: 1) To access an element at row i and column j, use triangle[i][j]. 2) Ensure i is within [0, len(triangle)-1] and j is within [0, len(triangle[i])-1]. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within the reverse iteration to get current and below rows. Common mistakes: using uniform indices for all rows (assuming all rows have the same length), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or misunderstanding the structure (e.g., thinking it's a matrix). Real code pattern: element = triangle[i][j].

examples.trace: Step 1: triangle[1] gives row [3,4]. Step 2: triangle[1][0] gives element 3.
Step 1: triangle[2] gives row [6,5,7]. Step 2: triangle[2][2] gives element 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Triangle as a List of Lists
ladders.desc: Representing a triangular structure using nested lists, the basic data structure for the problem.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding how a triangle can be represented in code as a list of lists, where each inner list corresponds to a row of the triangle. The intuition is that this jagged array structure naturally matches the triangular shape, with row i having i+1 elements. This representation allows easy access to rows and elements, which is essential for the dynamic programming approach. Step-by-step: 1) A triangle with n rows is stored as a list of n lists. 2) The first list (index 0) has 1 element, the second has 2, up to the nth having n elements. 3) Elements are typically integers representing costs or values. Time and space analysis are not primary here, but creation is O(n²) for n rows. Connection to L4: This structure is what we access elements from. Common mistakes: assuming all inner lists have the same length, incorrect indexing, or not validating the input structure. Real code pattern: triangle = [[2], [3,4], [6,5,7]] represents a triangle with 3 rows.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with an empty list: []. Step 2: Add first row: append([2]) → [[2]]. Step 3: Add second row: append([3,4]) → [[2],[3,4]]. Step 4: Add third row: append([6,5,7]) → [[2],[3,4],[6,5,7]].
Step 1: Create list: [[10]].

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP with Optimized Space O(n^2) O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an iterative bottom-up approach but store only the current row's minimum sums, reducing space usage by reusing a single array. This is optimal for large triangles where space efficiency is critical.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Triangle Minimum Path Sum with Bottom-Up DP and O(n) Space
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding minimum path sum from top to bottom of triangle using bottom-up DP with single array optimization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum path sum from the top element to any element in the bottom row of a triangle, where you can only move to adjacent numbers on the row below. The bottom-up DP approach with O(n) space works by starting from the second-to-last row and moving upward, calculating minimum path sums for each position. The key insight is that we only need to store one row of minimum sums at a time, since each calculation only depends on the row below it. We initialize an array with the values from the bottom row, then for each row above, we update the array by calculating min(dp[j], dp[j+1]) + triangle[i][j] for each position j. This reuses the same array space, reducing space complexity from O(n²) to O(n). The intuition is that the minimum path to any position (i,j) is the value at that position plus the minimum of the two possible paths from below (i+1,j) and (i+1,j+1). By processing from bottom to top, we ensure that when we calculate dp[j] for row i, dp[j] and dp[j+1] already contain the minimum path sums from positions (i+1,j) and (i+1,j+1) to the bottom. The final answer is dp[0] after processing all rows. Time complexity is O(n²) where n is the number of rows, as we process each of the n(n+1)/2 elements once. Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. Common mistakes include incorrectly handling the triangle structure (thinking it's a matrix), using top-down recursion without memoization (exponential time), or not updating the dp array correctly in-place (needs careful order to avoid overwriting needed values). The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete algorithm using the optimized space technique.

examples.trace:
Triangle: [[2],[3,4],[6,5,7],[4,1,8,3]]
Step 1: Initialize dp with bottom row: dp = [4,1,8,3]
Step 2: Process row 2 (index 2): [6,5,7]
  For j=0: dp[0] = min(dp[0], dp[1]) + 6 = min(4,1) + 6 = 1 + 6 = 7
  For j=1: dp[1] = min(dp[1], dp[2]) + 5 = min(1,8) + 5 = 1 + 5 = 6
  For j=2: dp[2] = min(dp[2], dp[3]) + 7 = min(8,3) + 7 = 3 + 7 = 10
  dp now: [7,6,10,3] (last element 3 unused for remaining rows)
Step 3: Process row 1 (index 1): [3,4]
  For j=0: dp[0] = min(dp[0], dp[1]) + 3 = min(7,6) + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9
  For j=1: dp[1] = min(dp[1], dp[2]) + 4 = min(6,10) + 4 = 6 + 4 = 10
  dp now: [9,10,10,3]
Step 4: Process row 0 (index 0): [2]
  For j=0: dp[0] = min(dp[0], dp[1]) + 2 = min(9,10) + 2 = 9 + 2 = 11
  dp now: [11,10,10,3]
Step 5: Return dp[0] = 11
Minimum path: 2→3→5→1 = 11

Triangle: [[-10]]
Step 1: Initialize dp with bottom row: dp = [-10]
Step 2: Only one row, so no processing needed.
Step 3: Return dp[0] = -10
Minimum path: -10

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bottom-Up DP Array Update for One Row
ladders.desc: Update the dp array for a given triangle row using values from the row below.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational step in the full solution. Given a dp array containing minimum path sums from the row below (size = row index + 2), and a current triangle row (list of integers at position i), we update dp in-place to store minimum path sums from the current row to the bottom. The algorithm processes each position j in the current row from left to right, calculating dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j+1]) + triangle[i][j]. The intuition is that dp[j] currently stores the minimum path sum from position (i+1,j) to the bottom, and dp[j+1] stores the minimum from (i+1,j+1) to the bottom. Since we can only move to adjacent positions below, the minimum path from (i,j) is its value plus the minimum of these two options. By updating from left to right, we ensure dp[j+1] hasn't been overwritten yet when we need it for position j. Time complexity for processing one row of length k is O(k). Space complexity is O(k+1) for the dp array. This feeds into L0 by being repeated for each row from bottom to top. Common mistakes include updating from right to left (which would require different formula), or not handling the dp array size correctly (it should be length of current row + 1). The real code pattern shows the in-place update loop.

examples.trace:
Initial dp: [4,1,8,3]
Row: [6,5,7], length = 3
Process j=0: dp[0] = min(dp[0], dp[1]) + 6 = min(4,1) + 6 = 1 + 6 = 7
  dp becomes: [7,1,8,3]
Process j=1: dp[1] = min(dp[1], dp[2]) + 5 = min(1,8) + 5 = 1 + 5 = 6
  dp becomes: [7,6,8,3]
Process j=2: dp[2] = min(dp[2], dp[3]) + 7 = min(8,3) + 7 = 3 + 7 = 10
  dp becomes: [7,6,10,3]
Final dp: [7,6,10,3]

Initial dp: [9,10,10,3]
Row: [2], length = 1
Process j=0: dp[0] = min(dp[0], dp[1]) + 2 = min(9,10) + 2 = 9 + 2 = 11
  dp becomes: [11,10,10,3]
Final dp: [11,10,10,3]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Two Adjacent Values Plus Current
ladders.desc: Calculate minimum path sum for a position using min of two values below plus current value.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental recurrence relation at the heart of the DP solution. For any position (i,j) in the triangle, the minimum path sum from that position to the bottom is triangle[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i+1,j+1)). The intuition is dynamic programming optimal substructure: the optimal path from (i,j) must go through either the left child (i+1,j) or right child (i+1,j+1), so the optimal solution is the current value plus the better of the two subproblems. This works because the triangle structure ensures only two possible moves downward. The base case is the bottom row where minPath = triangle value itself. This recurrence can be computed bottom-up by starting from the second-to-last row. Time complexity for computing one position is O(1). Space complexity depends on implementation. This feeds into L1 by being applied to each position in a row. Common mistakes include incorrect indices (off-by-one errors), or misunderstanding adjacency (thinking it's a grid with four directions). The real code pattern shows the simple formula.

examples.trace:
Position value: 6
Minimum of below values: min(4,1) = 1
Sum: 6 + 1 = 7
So minimum path sum from this position is 7

Position value: 2
Minimum of below values: min(9,10) = 9
Sum: 2 + 9 = 11
So minimum path sum from this position is 11

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Triangle Rows in Reverse
ladders.desc: Process triangle rows from bottom to top using indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves correctly traversing the triangle data structure from the last row to the first. Given a triangle as list of lists where triangle[i] has i+1 elements, we need to iterate i from len(triangle)-2 down to 0. The intuition is that bottom-up DP requires knowing solutions from the row below to compute the current row, so we must start from the base case (bottom row) and work upward. For each row i, we access triangle[i] which has length i+1, and we'll update values for positions j from 0 to i. This reverse iteration ensures that when we process row i, all values for row i+1 have already been computed. Time complexity for the iteration is O(n) for n rows. Space complexity is O(1) for indices. This feeds into L2 by providing the framework to apply the recurrence to each row. Common mistakes include incorrect range (going from 0 to n-1 instead of n-2 to 0), or mishandling the bottom row initialization. The real code pattern shows the for loop with range.

examples.trace:
Triangle has 4 rows (indices 0,1,2,3)
Bottom row is index 3: [4,1,8,3] (used for initialization)
We process from second-to-last row upward:
Process row index 2: [6,5,7]
Process row index 1: [3,4]
Process row index 0: [2]
Row index 3 is not processed (it's the base case)

examples.output: Row indices processed: (none, only one row)
examples.trace:
Triangle has 1 row (index 0)
Bottom row is index 0: [-10]
Since len(triangle)-2 = -1, no rows are processed in the loop
Only initialization of dp with bottom row needed

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and return the smaller value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the DP recurrence to choose between two possible paths. Given two numbers a and b, we need to determine min(a,b). The intuition is simple comparison: if a < b, return a; otherwise return b. For equal values, either can be returned. This is a constant-time O(1) operation with O(1) space. It feeds into L3 as part of calculating the recurrence for each position. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison logic or not handling negative numbers properly (standard comparison works). The real code pattern shows a simple conditional or built-in min function.

examples.trace:
Compare 4 and 1
4 > 1, so minimum is 1

Compare 9 and 10
9 < 10, so minimum is 9

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding List of Lists (2D Structure)
ladders.desc: Access elements in a nested list representing triangle rows.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves working with a triangle represented as a list of lists, where the outer list contains rows, and each inner list contains the values for that row. For example, triangle = [[2],[3,4],[6,5,7]] has 3 rows: row 0 has 1 element (2), row 1 has 2 elements (3,4), row 2 has 3 elements (6,5,7). To access an element at row i, position j, use triangle[i][j]. The intuition is that this data structure naturally represents the triangular shape where each row has one more element than the previous row. Key properties: len(triangle) gives number of rows; len(triangle[i]) gives number of elements in row i, which equals i+1 for 0-indexed rows. This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure we operate on. Common mistakes include treating it as a rectangular matrix (assuming all rows have same length), or incorrect indexing (forgetting that row indices start at 0). Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(n²) to store the triangle. The real code pattern shows basic list indexing.

examples.trace:
triangle is list of 3 rows
Row 0: [2]
Row 1: [3,4]
Row 2: [6,5,7]
Access triangle[1][0]:
  triangle[1] gets row 1: [3,4]
  [3,4][0] gets first element: 3
Result: 3

triangle is list of 1 row
Row 0: [-10]
Access triangle[0][0]:
  triangle[0] gets row 0: [-10]
  [-10][0] gets first element: -10
Result: -10
